text: The first commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism This article presents an exposition of the first commandment as found in the Heidelberg Catechism (HC). After a brief reconstruction of the HC1.1 wording in its 16th-century German and Latin sources, the article explores how Zacharias Ursinus, its principal author, was indebted to his Wittenberg mentor and hence reflects the teachings of Philipp Melanchthon, as documented in Melanchthon’s Loci Communes (LW 19.8, 143–69 and 266–96; LCC 19.88, 135–51 and 153–87) and Empfehlung der Heiligen Schrift bei den Konfessionen (LW 19. 370–73). Then, the treatise of the first commandment in Ursinus’ Smaller Catechism 1.29 and Larger Catechism 1.51–55 and his dogmatic lecturers on both Catechisms are taken into account, so as to understand more fully the structure and content of the first commandment in the Heidelberg Catechism.
explanation: The passage reads like a concise academic abstract with precise citations to specific works, which AI can imitate but could also be genuine scholarly prose.
